Honey never spoils. Archaeologists have found edible pots of it in ancient Egyptian tombs over three thousand years old.
Carrots were originally purple and white. The familiar orange carrot was cultivated by Dutch growers in the sixteenth century.
Ketchup began as a fermented fish sauce in Southeast Asia, with no tomatoes in it at all for centuries.
Nutmeg was once so valuable that the Dutch traded Manhattan to the British for a tiny nutmeg-producing island.
Pineapples take about two years to grow a single fruit, which is why they were once a symbol of wealth.
Bananas are berries, botanically speaking, but strawberries are not.
Peanuts are not nuts. They are legumes, more closely related to peas and lentils.
Chocolate was consumed as a bitter drink for thousands of years before anyone thought to make it solid and sweet.
Saffron is the most expensive spice by weight because each flower yields only three tiny threads picked by hand.
Worcestershire sauce is made from anchovies that are fermented for around eighteen months.
The fear of peanut butter sticking to the roof of your mouth has a name: arachibutyrophobia.
Apples float in water because roughly a quarter of their volume is air.
Ripe cranberries bounce like rubber balls, and growers once used a bounce test to sort the good ones.
Vanilla is the second most expensive spice after saffron because its orchids must be hand-pollinated.
A single strand of dried spaghetti is called a spaghetto.
Cashews grow attached to a fruit called a cashew apple, hanging beneath it like a comma.
Wild almonds are naturally poisonous. A single genetic mutation gave us the sweet almonds we eat today.
Espresso has less caffeine per serving than a mug of drip coffee, because the serving is so small.
The most expensive coffee comes from beans eaten and passed by a civet cat before roasting.
Decaffeinated coffee still contains a small amount of caffeine. It is never truly caffeine free.
Instant coffee was invented in the late nineteenth century and became a staple ration in both world wars.
Coffee was banned at various times in Mecca, Sweden, and Prussia over fears it stirred up rebellion.
A coffee cherry usually contains two beans, but sometimes just one rounded bean called a peaberry.
The word cappuccino comes from Capuchin friars, whose brown robes matched the color of the drink.
Finland drinks more coffee per person than any other country on Earth.
Black pepper was so prized in ancient Rome that it was used to pay ransoms and rents.
Cinnamon comes from the inner bark of trees, harvested by peeling and letting it curl as it dries.
Cloves are unopened flower buds, dried until hard, from an evergreen tree in Indonesia.
Chili peppers do not actually burn you. Capsaicin tricks your nerves into signaling heat that is not there.
The Carolina Reaper held the record as the hottest chili pepper for years before Pepper X dethroned it.
Birds cannot taste capsaicin, which is how chili plants spread their seeds far and wide.
Ginger and turmeric are close botanical cousins, both underground stems rather than true roots.
Wasabi served in most restaurants is usually dyed horseradish, since real wasabi is rare and costly.
Paprika, cayenne, and bell peppers all come from the same plant species, just different varieties.
Star anise, not the anise plant, is the main source of the flu drug Tamiflu.
Cardamom is the third most expensive spice in the world by weight.
Century eggs are preserved in clay and ash for weeks until the yolk turns dark green and creamy.
Surstromming, a Swedish fermented herring, is so pungent that some airlines ban the cans as a hazard.
Casu marzu, a Sardinian cheese, is intentionally infested with live maggots and is technically illegal to sell.
Balut is a developing duck embryo boiled and eaten in the shell, a popular street food in the Philippines.
Hakarl is Greenland shark meat fermented for months, since the fresh flesh is poisonous to eat.
Iceland's national dish includes ram testicles, sheep head, and fermented shark, served at midwinter.
Escamoles, sometimes called insect caviar, are harvested ant larvae eaten in Mexico.
Fugu, the Japanese pufferfish, contains a poison so deadly that chefs train for years to serve it safely.
Haggis, Scotland's national dish, is sheep organs cooked inside the animal's own stomach lining.
Vegemite is made from leftover brewer's yeast, a byproduct of beer production.
Stinky tofu, a Taiwanese street food, is fermented in brine that can smell like rotting garbage.
Durian is banned on public transit in Singapore because of its overpowering smell.
A Michelin star has nothing to do with food originally. The tire company created the guide to sell more tires.
Ancient Romans ate a fermented fish sauce called garum on almost everything, like a Roman ketchup.
The sandwich is named after the Earl of Sandwich, who wanted to eat without leaving the card table.
French fries likely originated in Belgium, not France, where fried potatoes were a winter staple.
The croissant is Austrian in origin, not French, first baked in Vienna as the kipferl.
Ice cream sundaes may have been invented to get around laws banning ice cream sodas on Sundays.
The Caesar salad was invented in Mexico by an Italian immigrant, and has no link to Julius Caesar.
Pizza margherita was reportedly created to honor an Italian queen, its colors matching the national flag.
Fortune cookies were popularized in America and are largely unknown as a tradition in China.
Chicken tikka masala is often called a British national dish, likely invented in the UK, not India.
The first soup is believed to date back around twenty thousand years, based on ancient cooking pottery.
Ancient Egyptians used moldy bread as an early antibiotic on wounds, long before penicillin was understood.
Bread was used as edible plates in the Middle Ages, called trenchers, then given to the poor after meals.
Ketchup was sold as medicine in the 1830s, marketed as a cure for indigestion and diarrhea.
Graham crackers and cornflakes were both invented to promote bland diets meant to curb sinful urges.
The tomato was long feared as poisonous in Europe because acidic juice leached lead from pewter plates.
Pretzels may have been shaped by monks to represent arms folded in prayer.
Margarine was invented for Napoleon the Third, who offered a prize for a cheap butter substitute.
The Popsicle was invented by an eleven-year-old who left a cup of soda with a stirring stick outside overnight.
Corn flakes were created by the Kellogg brothers by accident when they left cooked wheat out too long.
The microwave oven was discovered when a radar engineer noticed a chocolate bar melting in his pocket.
Champagne can hold about ninety pounds per square inch of pressure, roughly three times a car tire.
Bubbles in soda and beer form on tiny scratches and specks, not on perfectly smooth glass.
Water can boil and freeze at the same time at a precise point called the triple point.
Adding salt to water raises its boiling point only slightly, not nearly enough to cook food faster.
The Maillard reaction is why seared steak, toast, and roasted coffee all get their brown, savory flavor.
Onions make you cry because cutting them releases a gas that turns to mild sulfuric acid in your eyes.
Chilling onions before cutting slows the enzyme reaction that produces the tear-inducing gas.
Umami, the savory fifth taste, was scientifically identified in Japan in 1908 from seaweed broth.
Your tongue does not have separate zones for each taste. That famous taste map is a misreading of old research.
Cilantro tastes like soap to some people because of a gene affecting how they smell aldehydes.
Milk turns sour because bacteria convert its sugar into lactic acid, which curdles the proteins.
Cheese is essentially controlled spoilage. Different molds and bacteria create every distinct variety.
The holes in Swiss cheese are made by bacteria releasing carbon dioxide gas as the cheese ages.
Blue cheese gets its veins from a mold related to the one that produces penicillin.
Gelatin, the wobble in jelly, is made from collagen extracted from animal skin and bones.
Popcorn pops because water trapped inside each kernel turns to steam and bursts the hard shell.
It takes about twelve honeybees their entire lives to produce a single teaspoon of honey.
The largest pizza ever made stretched over one hundred and twenty feet across in Rome.
A single white truffle has sold at auction for well over one hundred thousand dollars.
The heaviest onion ever grown weighed almost nine kilograms, roughly the weight of a small dog.
One person once ate seventy six hot dogs and buns in ten minutes at a competitive eating contest.
The tallest stack of pancakes ever built rose more than a meter high.
Peppers are measured for heat on the Scoville scale, based on how much sugar water dilutes the burn.
The banana you eat today is a clone. Nearly all export bananas are a single variety called Cavendish.
Apples belong to the rose family, along with pears, cherries, plums, and almonds.
Rhubarb leaves are toxic and can be poisonous, even though the stalks are a beloved dessert filling.
A pineapple is not one fruit but dozens of berries fused together around a central core.
Kiwifruit was renamed from Chinese gooseberry by New Zealand growers for better export marketing.
Lobster was once considered prison food, so cheap it was fed to inmates and servants in colonial America.
Tea bags were invented by accident when a merchant sent tea samples in small silk pouches.
Green, black, white, and oolong teas all come from the same plant. The difference is how they are processed.
